## Security & Compliance

> ⚠️ LOCKED — These rules cannot be overridden.

### Injection Prevention

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Parameterized queries only
NEVER use string concatenation, template literals, or f-strings in SQL/NoSQL/LDAP/OS commands with user input. MUST use parameterized queries (`$1`, `?`, `:param`), prepared statements, or ORM bindings. Example: `db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1', [userId])` not `db.query(`SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ${userId}`)`. Injection attacks are trivially preventable.

### Data Privacy & PII

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never disable TLS/SSL verification
NEVER set `rejectUnauthorized: false`, `verify=False`, `InsecureSkipVerify: true`, or `NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0`. Flag as CRITICAL in all HTTP clients, database connections, and API calls. Use proper certificate chains; for local dev, use mkcert. Example: `https.request(url, {rejectUnauthorized: true})` not `false`.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit
MUST use TLS 1.2+ for all network communication and AES-256 (or equivalent) for storage. NEVER store passwords in plaintext or use MD5/SHA; MUST use bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2. NEVER implement custom cryptographic algorithms—use crypto, libsodium, or framework-provided libraries only. Example: `bcrypt.hash(password, 12)` not `sha256(password)`.

### Authorization & Access Control

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Authorization on every endpoint
ALWAYS verify server-side that the authenticated caller is authorized for the specific resource/operation. Example: confirm user_id matches resource owner before returning `/:id` data. NEVER rely on client-side guards. Prevents IDOR.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Never implement custom authentication
NEVER build custom password hashing, JWT validation, or session management. ALWAYS use Auth0, Firebase Auth, Cognito, or Clerk. Custom auth has timing attacks, weak token generation, and broken invalidation.

### Destructive Operations

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No unguarded destructive operations
NEVER execute DELETE, DROP, rm -rf, git push --force, or git reset --hard without explicit user confirmation. ALWAYS use soft deletes and include rollback scripts for migrations. Irreversible operations MUST require interactive confirmation before execution.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Database changes through migrations only
NEVER run direct ALTER TABLE, ad-hoc SQL, or manual schema changes. ALL schema changes MUST use migration tools (Drizzle, Prisma, Alembic, Flyway). Every migration requires a rollback script. Direct modifications bypass peer review and recovery.

### Error Exposure

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No internal details in error responses
NEVER expose stack traces, SQL, database errors, file paths, or internal IDs in API responses. MUST return: `{"error":{"code":"SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE","message":"user-safe text","requestId":"..."}}`. Use centralized error handler for all exceptions.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### PII in logs
NEVER log, expose in API responses, or store in queries: emails, phone numbers, SSNs, payment cards, auth tokens, or IP addresses. Use data masking (e.g., last-4 digits) or redaction. Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### secrets management
NEVER commit secrets (API keys, passwords, tokens) to version control. ALWAYS use environment variables or a secrets manager (e.g., .env.local, HashiCorp Vault). Scan commits for exposed secrets automatically.

## Architecture & Patterns

### Error Handling

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### No silent error swallowing
NEVER use empty catch blocks, bare `except: pass`, or `.catch(() => {})`. Every catch MUST handle the error (retry/fallback/log with context), re-throw it, or add a comment explaining why it's safe to ignore. Example: `catch(e) { logger.error('Payment retry', {orderId, error: e}); }` instead of `catch(e) {}`.

### Testing

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
#### Tests required for new logic
ALWAYS write tests for new functions, endpoints, or behavioral changes. Minimum: one happy-path test, one error case, one boundary condition. Place tests in the project's established test directory using its framework.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### input validation
MUST validate all inputs (query, body, path) with Zod schemas before processing. Example: `z.object({reference_id: z.string().uuid()})`. Reject invalid requests with 400 errors.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### repository pattern
MUST use repository classes (e.g., `WebhookRepository`, `ChargeRepository`) for all database access. NEVER call Prisma directly in service code. Example: inject `WebhookRepository` into service, call `repository.create()` instead of `prisma.webhook.create()`.

## Conventions

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### structured logging
ALWAYS log with structured JSON: `{timestamp, level, service, user_id, action, resource, status, error_code}`. Never use unstructured strings. Enable alerting and tracing on all logs.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### tailwind css usage
NEVER use inline styles. ALWAYS use Tailwind CSS classes for styling. Example: `className="fixed inset-0 bg-black/50 flex items-center justify-center"` not `style={{position: 'fixed'}}`. See `.cursorrules` for Tailwind conventions.

<!-- Source: canopy-mvp (org) -->
### error response format
ALWAYS return errors as `{ error: { code: "ERROR_CODE", message: "description" } }`. Example: `{ error: { code: "MISSING_PARAMETER", message: "reference_id is required" } }`. No plain text or custom formats.
